State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 14, 1976


Mr. R. J. Maloney
District Attorney, County
of Glenn
526 W. Sycamore Street
P.O. Box 430
Willows, California 95988

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 76-082

Dear Mr. Maloney:

     Thank you for your letter of August 20, 1976, requesting an opinion with respect to possible conflicts of interest occasioned by the responsibilities of the County Engineer-Surveyor. This letter is to inform you that the Commission will issue an opinion in response to the questions you have raised. It is our understanding that these questions involve the following matters: a determination of which acts of the County Engineer-Surveyor are purely ministerial; a determination of which acts of the County Engineer-Surveyor pose possible conflicts of interest; and a determination of whether, notwithstanding the existence of a conflict of interest, the County Engineer-Surveyor is "legally required to act" in certain circumstances within the meaning of Government Code Section 87101 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701. (Your letter refers to Government Code Section 3625(c)(2) which has been superseded by the Political Reform Act. Section 87101, however, establishes the same exception.)

     The Commission reserves the right to request additional information which the Commission or its staff deems relevant to the preparation of the opinion. Specifically, it may be necessary to obtain more facts about the specific circumstances of the County Engineer-Surveyor's rulings and their potential effects upon his private economic interests.

     With respect to the question of whether "it is reasonably foreseeable that [a] decision [of the Surveyor] will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally" on his economic interests, you should be guided,


Mr. R. J. Maloney
September 14, 1976
Page Two


pending issuance of the opinion, by the Commission's regulations,
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18701, 18702 and 18703, and by the
Commission's opinions Nos. 75-083 and 75-089, 1 FPPC Ops. 71 and
198, respectively, copies of which are enclosed. In addition, if
you need interim advice from the staff pending issuance of the
opinion or if you have any further questions, please contact
Delbert Spurlock, Chief of the Commission's Conflict of Interest
Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:plh

Enclosure


If there is text present in this document, it may be too faint to read accurately. You may want to provide a higher contrast or clearer version of the image.

OFFICE OF THE
R. J. MALONEY
DISTRICT ATTORNEY
DISTRICT ATTORNEY
526 W. SYCAMORE ST.
P.O. BOX 430
(916) 934-3779
WILLOWS, CALIFORNIA 95988

REFER TO SUBJECT                                              August 20, 1976

Conflict of Interest


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
           Chief, Conflict of Interest

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

     I am seeking your opinion on the following:

                              QUESTIONS

     1. Is the County Surveyor legally required to act
in light of the alternatives available under the circumstances
existing in Glenn County (3625(c)(2) Government Code)?

     2. Does the County Surveyor's certification pur-
suant to 8766 and 8767 of the Business and Professions Code
constitute a ministerial action only?

     3(a) If the answer to the above question is no,
does action taken thereby constitute an economic interest
by virtue of the County Surveyor's ruling upon competitor's
work (3625(a) Government Code)?

          (b) Does any action taken by the County Surveyor
constitute an economic interest by virtue of the County
Surveyor's ruling upon competitor's work?

     4. Does reference in 8765(a) Business and Professions
Code to a "public officer" exclude the County Surveyor himself?


Fair Political Practices Commission
August 20, 1976
Page Two


     5.  Does the preparation of maps for land levelling
and drainage permits by a surveying crew on the payroll of
the County Surveyor's private firm, but hired out to another
independent surveyor to do the work constitute an economic
interest by the County Surveyor in light of the ultimate
permit issuance required of the County Surveyor (County
Code 16.16 et.seg., attached as Exhibit "A")?

FACTS

     Glenn County has a population of approximately
20,000 and employs a local licensed engineer-surveyor as
the County Engineer doing primarily county road work pur-
suant to contract (attached as Exhibit "B").  As County
Engineer he is required by County Ordinance to serve as a
member of the Technical Advisory Committee to the County
Planning Commission, the recommendations of which are pre-
requisite to action by the Planning Commission regarding
approval of subdivision maps among other things.  He is
also the City of Willows Engineer and as such sits on the
City of Willows Technical Advisory Committee to the City
Planning Commission, performing duties similar to those
performed for the County.

     The engineering work performed pursuant to contract
for the County would require should the County choose to employ
rather than contract for these identical services, the engineer
himself, a three man survey party, an office engineering
draftsman and a secretary in addition to the purchase of
costly hardware such as nuclear compaction guages, electronic
distance measuring equipment, a computer, vehicles, etc.

     This same licensed engineer-surveyor is appointed
as County Surveyor by the Board of Supervisors at a salary of
$440.00 per month and in his individual capacity he owns an
engineering-surveying corporation operating in Glenn County.
Three members of this corporate staff are licensed engineers
and a fourth is a licensed land surveyor in addition to the
County Engineer-Surveyor himself.  There are two other
licensed and practicing engineers and three licensed and
practicing surveyors in Glenn County.  On various different
projects, some of these other engineers and surveyors not

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 20, 1976
Page Three


associated with the County Engineer-Surveyor's private firm
associate another. These local engineers and surveyors
together with the County Engineer-Surveyor in his private
capacity do an estimated 95% of the engineering and surveying
work performed in Glenn County. The quantity of surveying
work performed for the county by the County Surveyor is quarter
time only, constituting on the average approximately 10
hours per week. The county does not have the work or funds
to justify a full time surveyor or engineer.

ARGUMENT

Glenn County is of such a size and economy that a
full time surveyor is not practically or economically feasible
and no engineer employed by the county (as opposed to contracted
with for services) could provide the services required and now
being served by the county without more than a substantial
increase of personnel, equipment and commensurate cost to the
county. There are no other personnel in the county's employ
qualified to provide the required expertise and service.
Employment of other local qualified personnel would necessarily
be made under arrangements similar to those presently in
existence. Since there are so few qualified local individuals
and private practice is a virtual necessity, a conflict board
of a minimum of three local practitioners to act in conflict
cases would necessarily result in constant internal conflicts
because of the quantity of work performed within the county
by this relatively small number of persons. Hence, it seems
that the county surveyor-engineer is required to act in every
instance as a practical matter and disclosure pursuant to
statute is the means of avoiding the evils sought to be
avoided in the legislative scheme.

Since the body of local practitioners in these
fields is so limited in this county, any ruling by the County
Engineer-Surveyor, even seemingly affecting the entire surveying-
engineering industry in the county, is in reality affecting
only a small number of people all of whom are the County
Engineer-Surveyor's direct competitors. Additionally, a
ruling on an issue presented by one of these competitors to
the County Engineer-Surveyor on a specific project leaves
open the possibility that the County Engineer-Surveyor could
rule adversely to increase costs of a competitor. Increas-
benefit to the County Engineer-Surveyor in his private capacity.

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 20, 1976
Page Four


Hence, the size of the county and the number of practitioners
suggests that 95% of the time no action by the County Engineer-
Surveyor could be taken where a conflict did not exist and
no change of personnel would remedy the situation nor would the
same services be available to the county through employment of
its own personnel without prohibitive costs.

     The use of a county surveyor-engineer from an adjacent
county in conflict cases would also be prohibitive in costs
since 1) conflict cases can be expected as outlined above
approximately 95% of the time, 2) the other county would have
to be compensated, and 3) the Glenn County Engineer-Surveyor
would be 95% of the time unable to act and yet continue to be
compensated - a practical and economic impossibility for Glenn
County.

     Specifically with reference to records of survey
(8762 Business and Professions Code) any time any survey
work is done in Glenn County including surveying work done in
any city in Glenn County by the County Surveyor in his individual
capacity a potential material discrepancy exists and if found,
a record of survey must be filed after certification by the
County Surveyor. This is because the nature of the material
discrepancies is such that the discrepancies are not apparent
until the work is completed. Compliance however, is not neces-
sary but only desirable (8768 Business and Professions Code)
since the record is filed notwithstanding the disapproval of
the County Surveyor. Further, a literal interpretation of
8765(a) of the Business and Professions Code clearly suggests
no conflict exists (necessarily implying certification is
ministerial) when the County Surveyor himself as a "public
officer" and in that capacity observes a material discrepancy
which would otherwise require a record of survey and merely
files the data in his own office since as County Surveyor he
is a public officer. Disclosure, public inspection and
availability for such inspection is all that is required.

     In an attempt to avoid criticism until such time as
the Political Fair Practices Commission renders an opinion on
the above questions, the County Engineer-Surveyor has ceased
preparing any and all subdivision or parcel maps (with the
exception of two parcel maps considered work in progress which
are to be completed) which maps ultimately require approval

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 20, 1976
Page Five


by the Planning Commission. With respect to Records of
Survey, again in an attempt to be conservative until such time
as the Commission rules on the above questions, the County
Engineer-Surveyor has ceased preparing any records of survey,
which cessation and discontinuance is a substantial part of the
work performed by any surveyor, including the County Surveyor-
Engineer in his private capacity.

     Based on the above stated facts and argument, I
believe the Glenn County Engineer-Surveyor is required to act
under 3625(c)(2) of the Government Code in his various responsi-
bilities in this County under the existing circumstances; that
certification pursuant to 8766 & 8767 of the Business and Pro-
fessions Code is ministerial only; and that "public officer" as
used in 8765(a) of the Business and Professions Code includes the
County Engineer-Surveyor.

COMMENT

     Because the economic situations of many other small
California counties is not dissimilar to Glenn County and their
situations regarding the number of qualified local personnel
to economically accomplish the community's as well as the county's needs is
also not dissimilar to Glenn County, the answers to the above
questions could have a direct affect upon a large number of
small California counties.

     I respectfully request answers to the above questions
and your opinion on the issues raised thereby as soon as is
reasonably possible.

                                        Respectfully yours,

                                        [signature]

                                        R. J. MALONEY
                                        District Attorney

ILDING SETBACK LINES

16.12.140 Variance – Notification of action –
Appeal.
The board shall, after due consideration, and
within thirty days after the filing of the report,
accept in whole or in part the report of the
planning commission and make its
determination and shall notify the applicant of
the action of the board. If the applicant is
dissatisfied with the determination, the
applicant shall be entitled to a hearing before
the board. (Ord. 349 §8 (5), 1956.)

16.12.150 Violation – Penalty.
Any person, firm or corporation who erects,
locates, installs, digs or constructs or causes to
be erected, located, installed, dug or
constructed, any building, well, excavation, or
subsurface structure or who enlarges or extends
a nonconforming use, contrary to the provisions
of this chapter is guilty of a misdemeanor, and
upon conviction thereof shall be punished by a
fine of not more than three hundred dollars or
by imprisonment in the county jail for a period
of not more than ninety days, or by both a
fine and imprisonment. Each day during which
any such building or enlargement or extension
of such building structure, well, excavation or
subsurface structure is permitted to continue
shall constitute a separate and distinct offense
and each offense shall be punishable as in this
section provided. (Ord. 349 §9, 1956.)

Chapter 16.16

LEVELLING OF LAND –
DRAINAGE CHANGES

Sections:
16.16.010 Necessity of regulations.
16.16.020 Levelling – Permit required.
16.16.030 Grading – Permit required.
16.16.040 Permit – Application –
Form and contents.
16.16.050 Permit – Application – Maps.
16.16.060 Permit – Application and
plans – Review.
16.16.070 Permit – Application –
Restrictions.
16.16.080 Permit – Issuance.
16.16.090 Permit – Refusal – Appeal –
Hearing.

16.16.100 Violation – Penalty.

16.16.010 Necessity of regulations.
The board finds that certain regulations
governing the levelling of land, the obstruction
of natural drainage, and the control of irrigation
and drainage water resulting therefrom are
necessary for the protection of roads, highways
and other public properties and improvements
pertaining to public health, safety, convenience
and general welfare. (Ord. 420 §1, 1963.)

16.16.020 Levelling – Permit required.
No person, firm or corporation shall
hereafter level, cause to be levelled, or
commence land levelling operations upon any
land area in one ownership of a total of one or
more acres, for farming, agricultural or
horticultural purposes, or to move, excavate,
remove, dredge, pile, stockpile or otherwise
change or cause to change the natural course of
any channel or waterway, unless and until a
permit has first been secured for such operation
from the county surveyor as hereinafter
provided. (Ord. 491 §1 (part), 1967: Ord. 420 §
2 (a), 1963.)

16.16.030 Grading – Permit required.
No person, firm or corporation shall grade,
cause to be graded or commence grading
operations on any parcel regardless of size,
preparatory to the construction of commercial
buildings or structures, off-street parking areas,
or to move, excavate, remove, dredge, pile,
stockpile or otherwise change the natural course
of any channel or waterway or to otherwise
increase or accelerate the flow of drainage water
onto the road or highway from any lot or parcel
unless and until a permit has first been secured
for such operation from the county surveyor, as
hereinafter provided. (Ord. 491 §1 (part), 1967:
Ord. 420 §2 (b), 1963.)

16.16.040 Permit – Application – Form and
contents.
The application for a permit shall be in
writing upon a form furnished by the county,
signed by the applicant and filed with the
county surveyor and shall be accompanied by
such of the following as may be required by the

132


county surveyor:
A. Plans, profiles, maps and other data as may
be required to show the present and
proposed work;
B. When land levelling is involved, it shall
include a plat of existing topography with a
one-foot contour interval, showing existing
drainage and water courses, adjacent roads,
highways, ditches, bridges, culverts and such
other data as may be required by the county
surveyor;
C. A map showing proposed finished levelled
area indicating finished elevations, irrigation
water source, and drainage features as called
for herein, may be shown on same map
called for in subsection B of this section;
D. A filing fee of five dollars for the first one to
five acres, plus one dollar for each additional
ten acres or portion thereof in excess of five
acres, which shall be deposited in the general
fund (Ord. 420 §3, 1963.)

16.16.050 Permit—Application—Maps.
Maps and other drawings shall be drafted to
a scale which will clearly set forth the intended
work and shall be legibly drawn and shall be
subject to approval of the county surveyor.
(Ord. 420 §4, 1963.)

16.16.060 Permit—Application and plans—
Review.
The application and plans shall be reviewed
by the county surveyor to insure that the
proposed work will not result in violating
existing laws, will not jeopardize public property
or improvements and will not endanger the
public health, safety, convenience and general
welfare. (Ord. 552 § 1, 1972; Ord. 420 § 5,
1963.)

16.16.070 Permit—Application—
Restrictions.
No application will be considered unless the
same provides that where natural watercourses
are proposed to be changed in size, grade,
capacity, location or otherwise, the proposed
new or altered channel shall in all cases be at
least equal in capacity to the original natural
watercourse and shall result in drainage water
resuming its natural course, upon leaving the

area where the work is proposed. (Ord. 420 § 6,
1963.)

16.16.080 Permit—Issuance.
The county surveyor shall, within a
reasonable time, not to exceed fifteen days,
after the filing of an application and plans, issue
a permit subject to such conditions as appear
necessary, or shall deny the permit, stating the
reasons therefor in writing. (Ord. 420 § 7,
1963.)

16.16.090 Permit—Refusal—Appeal—
Hearing.
In the event of refusal by the county
surveyor to grant a permit, or in the event of the
issuance of a conditional permit, the terms of
which are not satisfactory to the applicant, the
applicant may apply to the board for a review of
the action taken by the county surveyor. Such
application to the board shall be in writing, and
shall be filed with the clerk of the board, who
shall set the matter for hearing within fifteen
days after the receipt of the notice and shall
immediately notify the county surveyor of the
request for review. The board shall, within
fifteen days after hearing the report presented
by the applicant, approve, conditionally approve
or reject the application for a permit. The
determination of the board shall be final. (Ord.
420 §8, 1963.)

16.16.100 Violation—Penalty.
Any person, firm or corporation violating
the provisions of this chapter is guilty of a
misdemeanor, and shall be punishable by a fine
not in excess of five hundred dollars or by
imprisonment in the county jail not exceeding
six months, or by both such fine and
imprisonment in the county jail. Each day that
violation of this chapter continues shall be
considered a separate offense. (Ord. 420 § 9,
1963.)

THIS CONTRACT AND AGREEMENT made and entered into this

28th day of June, 1965, by and between COUNTY OF GLENN, a politi-

cal subdivision of the State of California, as party of the first

part, sometimes hereinafter referred to and designated as "County",

and THOMAS E. LANDON, as party of the second part, sometimes

hereinafter referred to and designated as "Contractor",

WITNESSETH:

WHEREAS, the Contractor is a duly qualified and licensed

surveyor and civil engineer; and

WHEREAS, it is the desire of the County to engage the

services of the Contractor in connection with engineering to be

performed for the County and particularly the Glenn County Road

Department;

NOW, THEREFORE, it is hereby mutually AGREED by and

between the parties hereto as follows:

1. Contractor hereby agrees to furnish and perform

all engineering design, right of way work, surveying, drafting,

inspection and construction engineering as required or requested

by the Board of Supervisors of the County of Glenn or Road

Commissioner on County projects that are constructed solely by

County personnel. Contractor may perform all of his obligations

hereunder by and through his professional corporation, namely,

LANDON ENGINEERING AND SURVEYING, INC., provided that at all

times during the term he is the majority stockholder and

principal officer of such corporation. (July 1, 1973)

Hence, the size of the county and the number of practitioners
suggests that 95? of the time no action by the County Engineer-
Surveyor could be taken where a conftict did not exist and
no change of personnel- would remedy the situaEl-on nor would the
same servi.ces be available to the county through employment of
its own personnel without prohibitive costs.
The use of a county surveyor-engineer from an adjacent
county in conflict cases would also be prohibitive in costs
since 1) conflict cases can be expected as outl j-ned above
approximately 95* of the time, 2) the other county wouLd have
to be compensated, and 3) the Glenn County Engineer-Surveyor
would be 95t of the time unable to act and yet continue to be
compensated - a practical and economic impossibility for clenn
County .
. 
Specifical-l-y with reference to records of survey
(8762 Business and Professions Code) any time any survey
work is done in Glenn County including surveying work done in
any cj-ty in Glenn County ky the County Surveyor in his individual
capacity a potential material discrepancy exists and if found,
a record of survey must be filed after certification by the
County Surveyor. This is because the nature of the material
discrepancies is such that the discrepancies are not apparent
until the work is compl-eted. Compliance however, is nol neces-
sary but only desirable (8768 Business and professions Code)
since the record is filed notwithstanding the disapproval of
the County Surveyor. Further, a literal 
interpretation of
8765 (a) of the Business and professions Code clearly suggests
no conflict exists (necessarily implying certification ii
mj.nisterial) when the County Surveyor himself as a ,'public
officer" and in that capacity observes a material diicrepancy
which would otherwise require a record of survey and merely
files the data in his own office since as County Surveyor he
is a public officer. 
Disclosure, public inspecLion ana the
availability 
for such inspection is all that is required.
In an attempt to avoid criticism until such time as
the Politica1 Fair practices Commission renders an opinion on
the above questions, the County Engineer-surveyor ha! ceased
preparing any and all subdivision or parcel maps (with the
exception of two parcel maps considered work in progress which
are to be completed) which maps ultimately require approval


v

August 20, 197 5

by the Planning Commlssion. with respect to Records of
Survey, again in an attempt to be conservative until such time
as the Commission rules on the above questions, the County
Engineer-Surveyor has ceased preparing any records of survey,
which cessation and discontinuance is a substantial part of the
work performed by any surveyor, including the County Surveyor-
Engineer in his prj,vate capacity.
Based on the above stated facts and argument, I
believe the Glenn County Engineer-Surveyor is required to act
under 3625(c) (2) of the Government Code in his various responsi-
bilities 
in this County under the existing circumstances; that
certification 
pursuant to 8766 & 8767 of the Business and Pro-
fessions Code is ministeriat only; and that "public officer" as
used in 8765(a) of the Business and Professions Code includes the
County Engineer-Surveyo!.
COI'IMENT
Because the economic situations of many other smaIl
California counties is not dissirnilar to Glenn County and their
sj-tuations regarding the nrmber of qualified local personnel
to accompl-ish the community's as well as the county's needs is
also not dissimilar to G1enn county, the answers to the above
sections.question_synthetic: The Glenn County District Attorney seeks FPPC guidance on whether a County Engineer-Surveyor who operates a private engineering-surveying firm has conflicts of interest when making decisions affecting competitors in a small county with limited qualified personnel. The questions address whether certain acts are ministerial, whether conflicts exist, and whether the County Surveyor is legally required to act despite potential conflicts.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (main content) and the FPPC's acknowledgment/response indicating an opinion will be issued. The incoming request is substantially longer and contains detailed Questions, Facts, Argument, and Comment sections. The FPPC response (dated September 14, 1976) acknowledges the questions and indicates the Commission will prepare an opinion, but does not provide the actual opinion/conclusion. Extracted question text from incoming request section.

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 September 14, 1976 Mr. R. J. Maloney District Attorney, County of Glenn 526 W. Sycamore Street P.O. Box 430 Willows, California 95988 Re: Opinion Request No. 76-082 Dear Mr. Maloney: Thank you for your letter of August 20, 1976, requesting an opinion with respect to possible conflicts of interest occasioned by the responsibilities of the County Engineer-Surveyor. This letter is to inform you that the Commission will issue an opinion in response to the questions you have raised. It is our understanding that these questions involve the following matters: a determination of which acts of the County Engineer-Surveyor are purely ministerial; a determination of which acts of the County Engineer-Surveyor pose possible conflicts of interest; and a determination of whether, notwithstanding the existence of a conflict of interest, the County Engineer-Surveyor is "legally required to act" in certain circumstances within the meaning of Government Code Section 87101 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701. (Your letter refers to Government Code Section 3625(c)(2) which has been superseded by the Political Reform Act. Section 87101, however, establishes the same exception.) The Commission reserves the right to request additional information which the Commission or its staff deems relevant to the preparation of the opinion. Specifically, it may be necessary to obtain more facts about the specific circumstances of the County Engineer-Surveyor's rulings and their potential effects upon his private economic interests. With respect to the question of whether "it is reasonably foreseeable that [a] decision [of the Surveyor] will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally" on his economic interests, you should be guided, Mr. R. J. Maloney September 14, 1976 Page Two pending issuance of the opinion, by the Commission's regulations, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18701, 18702 and 18703, and by the Commission's opinions Nos. 75-083 and 75-089, 1 FPPC Ops. 71 and 198, respectively, copies of which are enclosed. In addition, if you need interim advice from the staff pending issuance of the opinion or if you have any further questions, please contact Delbert Spurlock, Chief of the Commission's Conflict of Interest Division. Sincerely, [signature] Michael Bennett Executive Director MB:plh Enclosure If there is text present in this document, it may be too faint to read accurately. You may want to provide a higher contrast or clearer version of the image. OFFICE OF THE R. J. MALONEY DISTRICT ATTORNEY DISTRICT ATTORNEY 526 W. SYCAMORE ST. P.O. BOX 430 (916) 934-3779 WILLOWS, CALIFORNIA 95988 REFER TO SUBJECT August 20, 1976 Conflict of Interest Fair Political Practices Commission 1100 K Street Building P. O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95814 Attention: Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr. Chief, Conflict of Interest Dear Mr. Spurlock: I am seeking your opinion on the following: QUESTIONS 1. Is the County Surveyor legally required to act in light of the alternatives available under the circumstances existing in Glenn County (3625(c)(2)
embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the Glenn County District Attorney (August 20, 1976) asking the FPPC for an opinion on potential conflicts of interest arising from a County Engineer-Surveyor who simultaneously operates a private engineering-surveying corporation in a small county. The District Attorney argues that the small population and limited number of qualified practitioners make it practically impossible to avoid conflicts and that disclosure is the appropriate remedy.
